Chief Complaint:
s/p Fall 16ft

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1973-01-23 - 1. Open reduction internal fixation of right ulna
with 3.5mm plate. 2. Closed reduction of radial head
dislocation.


History of Present Illness:
37 yo male s/p 16 ft. fall from a ladder.  There was no reported
LOC and
no witnesses present.  He was taken to an area hopsital where
found to have T9 and L1 fractures without canal compromise. He
was then transported to Mitchell County Hospital Health Systems for further care. Upon admission
he denies any incontinence, weakness, numbness and/or tingling
in his extremities .


Past Medical History:
EtOH abuse

+EtOH

Noncontributory

CT Head 2013-05-19
FINDINGS: There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, shift of
normally
midline structures, hydrocephalus, major or minor vascular
territorial
infarction. The density values of the brain parenchyma appear
maintained.
The soft tissues and osseous structures are intact. The
paranasal sinuses and
mastoid air cells appear well aerated.

IMPRESSION: No acute intracranial hemorrhage.

CT cervical spine 2013-05-19
IMPRESSION:

1. Possible epidural hematoma posterior to the C2 and C3
vertebral bodies.
Correlation with cervical MRI is recommended.

2. Possible anterior wedging of the T1 vertebral body,
incompletely
visualized, likely acute. Thoracic MRI is recommended.


MR Cervical, Thoracic and Lumbar Spine 2004-09-10
IMPRESSION: Findings indicative of acute right-sided
subligamentous disc
herniation at C2-3 level with elevation of the posterior
longitudinal
ligament. No evidence of ligamentous disruption or abnormal
signal seen
within the vertebral bodies. No evidence of intraspinal hematoma
or extrinsic
spinal cord compression.

THORACIC SPINE:

TECHNIQUE: T1, T2 and inversion recovery sagittal and T2 axial
images of the
thoracic spine were acquired.

FINDINGS: There is acute mild compression fracture of T9
vertebra identified.
No significant retropulsion or spinal stenosis seen. No evidence
of
disruption of the anterior or posterior longitudinal ligament or
ligamentum
flavum identified. Mild soft tissue prominence in the region
indicates
minimal paraspinal hematoma.

The spinal cord shows normal intrinsic signal without extrinsic
compression.

IMPRESSION: Mild acute compression fracture of T9 vertebra
without
retropulsion. Other findings as described above.

LUMBAR SPINE:

TECHNIQUE: T1, T2 and inversion recovery sagittal and T2 axial
images of the
lumbar spine were acquired.

FINDINGS: There is mild-to-moderate compression fracture of L1
vertebra
identified. There is minimal retropulsion seen of the
posterior-superior
margin of the compressed vertebral body without spinal stenosis.
There is no
significant thecal sac compression seen.

From L1-2 to L3-4, no significant abnormalities are identified.

At L4-5, disc bulging and degenerative changes are seen.

At L5-S1 level, bilateral spondylolysis of L5 is seen with grade
1
spondylolisthesis of L5 over S1. There is moderate narrowing of
the left and
severe narrowing of the right foramen seen at this level
secondary to
uncovering of disc bulging and spondylolisthesis.

The paraspinal soft tissues are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Acute mild-to-moderate compression fracture of L1 vertebra
with minimal
retropulsion without spinal stenosis or high-grade thecal sac
compression.

2. Bilateral grade 1 spondylolisthesis of L5 over S1 with severe
right-sided
and moderate right-sided foraminal narrowing.

Left Ventricle - Cardiac Index:  3.31  >= 2.0 L/min/M2
Left Ventricle - Lateral Peak E':  0.11 m/s  > 0.08 m/s
Left Ventricle - Septal Peak E':  0.12 m/s  > 0.08 m/s
Left Ventricle - Ratio E/E':  7  < 15
Aorta - Sinus Level:  *3.7 cm  <= 3.6 cm
Aorta - Arch:  *3.2 cm  <= 3.0 cm
Aortic Valve - Peak Velocity:  1.6 m/sec  <= 2.0 m/sec
Aortic Valve - LVOT VTI:  24
Aortic Valve - LVOT diam:  2.1 cm
Mitral Valve - E Wave:  0.8 m/sec
Mitral Valve - A Wave:  0.6 m/sec
Mitral Valve - E/A ratio:  1.33
Mitral Valve - E Wave deceleration time:  181 ms  140-250 ms
TR Gradient (+ RA = PASP):  22 mm Hg  <= 25 mm Hg

Findings
LEFT ATRIUM: Normal LA and RA cavity sizes.

RIGHT ATRIUM/INTERATRIAL SEPTUM: Normal IVC diameter (<2.1cm)
with >55% decrease during respiration (estimated RA pressure
(0-5mmHg).

LEFT VENTRICLE: Normal LV wall thickness, cavity size and
regional/global systolic function (LVEF >55%). Estimated cardiac
index is normal (>=2.5L/min/m2). TDI E/e' < 8, suggesting normal
PCWP (<12mmHg). Transmitral Doppler and TVI c/w normal LV
diastolic function. No resting LVOT gradient.

RIGHT VENTRICLE: Normal RV chamber size and free wall motion.

AORTA: Mildly dilated aortic sinus. Mildly dilated aortic arch.

AORTIC VALVE: Normal aortic valve leaflets (3). No AS. No AR.

MITRAL VALVE: Normal mitral valve leaflets with trivial MR.
Normal LV inflow pattern for age.

TRICUSPID VALVE: Normal tricuspid valve leaflets with trivial
TR. Normal PA systolic pressure.

PULMONIC VALVE/PULMONARY ARTERY: Normal pulmonic valve leaflet.
No PS. Physiologic PR.

PERICARDIUM: Trivial/physiologic pericardial effusion.
Conclusions
The left atrium and right atrium are normal in cavity size. The
estimated right atrial pressure is 0-5 mmHg. Left ventricular
wall thickness, cavity size and regional/global systolic
function are normal (LVEF >55%). The estimated cardiac index is
normal (>=2.5L/min/m2). Tissue Doppler imaging suggests a normal
left ventricular filling pressure (PCWP<12mmHg). Transmitral
Doppler and tissue velocity imaging are consistent with normal
LV diastolic function. Right ventricular chamber size and free
wall motion are normal. The aortic root is mildly dilated at the
sinus level. The aortic valve leaflets (3) appear structurally
normal with good leaflet excursion and no aortic regurgitation.
The mitral valve appears structurally normal with trivial mitral
regurgitation. The estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure
is normal. There is a trivial/physiologic pericardial effusion.

IMPRESSION: Mildly dilated ascending aorta. No valvular
pathology or pathologic flow identified.

CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS:
Based on 1998 AHA endocarditis prophylaxis recommendations, the
echo findings indicate prophylaxis is NOT recommended. Clinical
decisions regarding the need for prophylaxis should be based on
clinical and echocardiographic data.




Brief Hospital Course:
He was admitted to the Trauma Service. He was pancytopenic upon
admission which prompted a Hematology-Oncology consult; it was
felt that the pancytopenia was consistent with ethanol toxicity
on the bone marrow. His PLT count was low; platelet counts can
be as low as 10,000/UL with heavy ethanol consumption and given
his history of EtOH abuse would qualify as this. The anemia was
felt to be due to bone marrow toxicity and that he may have a
component of iron deficiency if he had gastritis from ethanol.
He was started on PPI during his hospital course.
It was felt that given this it was likely due to alcohol
toxicity on the bone marrow, that his CBC should  recover in the
next several days. If not a bone marrow biopsy was recommended.
He will need to follow up with his primary care doctor and also
with Hematology-Oncology for ongoing monitoring.

Neurosurgery Spine and Orthopedics were consulted given his
injuries. His spine fractures were managed nonoperative and he
was fitted for a TLSO brace which needs to be worn while out of
bed. Operative intervention for his right Montaggio fracture was
delayed because of his pancytopenia; he did eventually go to the
operating room for definitive repair of this fracture. He was
placed in a splint postoperatively and will remain non weight
bearing until clearance form Orthopedics. He will follow up in
their clinic as an outpatient.

Infectious Disease was also consulted given his fevers and
positive blood cultures with MSSA and pneumococci on 08-19. A
TTE and Nafcillin 1 gram q 6 hours was recommended. A PICC line
was placed for this. He was later changed to oral antibiotics
prior to discharge and the PICC was discontinued.

He was evaluated by Physical therapy and was eventually cleared
for home. Social work was also consulted early on given his
history with alcohol use; he expressed interest in getting help
with his substance issues and was provided information on
outpatient alcohol and drug treatments.

Medications on Admission:
None

Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Right Montaggio fracture
C2-C3 epidural hematoma
T1, T9, L1 vertebral body fractures, (L1 w/ 3mm retropulsion)
Right lower lobe contusion
Pancytopenia


Discharge Condition:
Good

Discharge Instructions:
Please return to the Emergency room if you develop headaches,
dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, fevers, severe
abdominal pain or distention, persistent nausea or vomiting,
inability to eat or drink, or any other symptoms which are
concerning to you.

DO NOT drive, drink, operate heavy machinery or take any illicit
drugs while on narcotics.

DO NOT bear any weight on your right arm.

Medications: Resume your home medications. You should take a
stool softener, Colace 100 mg twice daily as needed for
constipation. Pain medication may make you drowsy. No driving
while taking pain medicine.


Followup Instructions:
Please call the office of Dr. Adam Charles Robbins MD MD(neurosurgery)
at (241-235-166 to schedule a follow-up appointment for the
management of your TLSO brace

Please call the office of Dr.Hall (Orthopaedic Surgery) at
(871-13-8940 to schedule a follow-up appointment within 1 week
for your right arm fracture.

You will need to follow up with Hematology-Oncology in the next
1-2 weeks for moitoring of your hematology studies. You may need
to undergo a bone marrow biospsy at some point. Please call
(605-68-7632 to schedule the appointment.

You will need to follow up with your primary care provider for
monitoring of your hematology studies. If you do not have a
primary care doctor and would like to contiunue your care at
Memorial Hospital Temple Community Hospital Medical Center please call 817-784-9029 to
request the names of physicans taking on new patients.
